lesson.title: Working with text
masteryOutcomes: Define a "data type" as a category of data that determines what you can do with it.
Create a string variable by enclosing text in single (') or double (") quotes.
Combine two strings using the + operator (concatenation).
Predict the output when printing string variables.

concept: Strings as a data type: creating string variables with quotes, combining them with +, and predicting printed output
prerequisites: Writing and running a program with print()
Printing multiple values with commas and using + and * inside print()

explanations.text: Think of data types like containers in a kitchen: a jar for cookies, a bottle for juice. What's inside decides what you can do with it — you drink juice, you bite cookies. In Python, text lives in a container called a string. You make a string by putting text in quotes, like "hello", and you can glue two strings together with + to make a bigger one.

A data type is a category of data that determines what you can do with it. Text in Python is the string type. You create a string by wrapping characters in single quotes ('hi') or double quotes ("hi") — both work the same. You can store a string in a variable with =, like name = "Ada", and combine strings with the + operator: greeting = "Hello, " + name. Printing greeting shows Hello, Ada — the two pieces glued together exactly, including any spaces you typed.

Every value in Python has a type, and the type decides which operations are valid. For strings (type str), the + operator means concatenation: it produces a new string made of the left string's characters followed immediately by the right string's characters — no space is added unless one is inside the quotes. So "Hello," + "Ada" gives "Hello,Ada", while "Hello, " + "Ada" gives "Hello, Ada". A variable like name = "Ada" is just a label pointing at that string, so print(name) shows the stored text Ada, not the word name. Trying to + a string with a number raises a TypeError, because the type determines what's allowed.

examples:
pet = 'Rex'
print("My dog is called " + pet)

hintLadder.text: Text in Python must go inside quotes — check whether every piece of text you wrote is wrapped in ' or ".
To store the text, use a variable: write a name, then =, then the quoted text. What would that line look like?
To glue two strings together, put + between them. Remember: + adds no space by itself — where would a space need to live?
Read your line left to right the way Python does: each quoted piece and each variable's stored text get joined in order. Now run it and compare to what you predicted.

misconceptions.trigger: Predicts that "Hello," + "Ada" prints Hello, Ada with a space.
misconceptions.correction: The + operator glues strings with nothing in between. To get a space, it must be inside the quotes: "Hello, " + "Ada".

misconceptions.trigger: Writes print(name) and expects it to print the word name, or writes print("name") expecting it to print Ada.
misconceptions.correction: Without quotes, name is a variable and Python prints what's stored inside it (Ada). With quotes, "name" is just the literal text name. Quotes mean literal text; no quotes means look up the variable.

misconceptions.trigger: Mixes quote styles, e.g. 'Hello" or thinks single and double quotes make different strings.
misconceptions.correction: Single and double quotes create identical strings — but each string must start and end with the SAME quote character: 'hi' or "hi", never 'hi".

misconceptions.trigger: Tries "Age: " + 30 and is confused by the error.
misconceptions.correction: That's the data type idea in action: + between a string and a number isn't allowed, so Python raises a TypeError. Make both sides strings — "Age: " + "30" — or use a comma inside print like you did last lesson.
Without quotes, Python treats Ada as another variable to look up and errors because it doesn't exist. Wrap the text: name = "Ada".

checks.prompt: Create a variable called city that stores the name of a city you'd love to visit, then print it.
acceptanceCriteria: Assigns a quoted string to a variable named city
The string uses matching quotes (both single or both double)
Calls print(city) without quotes around the variable name
Running it outputs the city name
checks.passFeedback: You stored text in a variable and printed it back — that's a real string variable at work.
checks.failFeedback: Check two things: the text must be in matching quotes when you store it, and print(city) needs no quotes so Python looks up the variable. Fix and run again.

checks.prompt: Make two variables, first = "Good " and second = "morning", then print them combined with + so the output is exactly: Good morning
acceptanceCriteria: Defines both variables as quoted strings
Uses the + operator to combine them
Running it outputs exactly Good morning (with one space, which comes from inside a string)
checks.passFeedback: Concatenation unlocked — you built one string out of two, and the space landed exactly where you put it.
checks.failFeedback: Almost — remember + adds no space of its own. Is the space inside one of your quoted strings? Run it and check the output character by character.

practice.prompt: Create a variable animal with your favorite animal, and a variable sound with the noise it makes. Then use + to print one sentence like: The dog says woof (build it from strings and your two variables).
practice.starterCode:
animal = 
sound = 
print("The " + animal + " says " + sound)
acceptanceCriteria: Both variables are assigned quoted strings
The print line combines strings and variables using +
Runs without errors and outputs a readable sentence with correct spaces

practice.prompt: In your own words: what is a data type, and why does Python care whether something is a string or a number?
rubric: Defines a data type as a category of data that determines what you can do with it
Gives a concrete consequence, e.g. + joins strings but adds numbers, or string + number causes an error

practice.prompt:
What does this program print?

first = "sun"
second = "shine"
print(first + second)
practice.explanation: print looks up the text stored in each variable and + glues them with nothing in between, so the output is sunshine — no space, no quotes, no variable names.

pedagogyRules: Before every run of concatenation code, ask the student to say out loud what they predict the output will be — prediction before execution is the core habit of this lesson.
When a space is missing or doubled in output, don't fix it for them: ask 'where does the space live in your code?' and let them find it.
If the student says they already know strings, respect it: skip straight to the prediction check with first + second and advance if they nail it — no re-drilling quotes.
Anchor everything to the definition: whenever an error involving types appears, name it as 'the data type deciding what's allowed', not as a random rule.
Keep the student typing in the editor and pressing Run themselves; never confirm output they haven't actually run.
One idea per turn: quotes first, then variables, then +, then prediction — never all four in one explanation.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when the actual Run output matches the prediction exactly, including spaces. If output shows sunshine but the student predicted sun shine, treat the mismatch as the teaching moment, not a pass.

Read the real interpreter output. If it says TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str, explain it as string + number being disallowed; if it says NameError, explain the missing quotes made Python look up a variable. Never invent a different cause.
If the student is lost, drop back one layer: get a single quoted string printing first, then add a variable, then add +. One hint from the ladder at a time, with a run between each.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student questions the value of the lesson
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Give one concrete use — every app that greets you by name ("Hi, Sam!") is string concatenation — then immediately set a tiny task: build a greeting with their own name using +.

chunks.text: A data type is a category of data that determines what you can do with it. Text in Python is the string type (str), and numbers are a different type. The type decides which operations are allowed: + between two strings glues them together, + between two numbers adds them, and + between a string and a number raises a TypeError.

You create a string by wrapping text in quotes: either single quotes ('hello') or double quotes ("hello") — both make the exact same string, but the opening and closing quote must match. You store a string in a variable with =, for example name = "Ada". Then print(name) — no quotes — prints the stored text: Ada. With quotes, print("name") would print the literal word name instead.

The + operator between strings is called concatenation: it joins them into one new string with nothing added in between. "sun" + "shine" gives sunshine. If you want a space, it must be inside the quotes: "Hello, " + "Ada" gives Hello, Ada because the space after the comma is part of the first string.

Worked example — predict before you run:

first = "Good "
second = "morning"
print(first + second)

Python looks up first (which holds "Good " with a trailing space), looks up second ("morning"), glues them with +, and prints: Good morning. Type it in the editor, say your prediction out loud, then press Run to check.

Common errors and what they mean: name = Ada (no quotes) gives NameError because Python looks for a variable called Ada. "Age: " + 30 gives TypeError because you can't concatenate a string with a number — make both strings ("Age: " + "30") or use a comma in print. 'hi" gives a SyntaxError because the quotes don't match.

celebration.headline: You're officially working with data types.
celebration.recap: You learned that a data type decides what you can do with data, created string variables in quotes, glued strings together with +, and predicted output before running — like a real programmer.
celebration.unlocked: Next: numbers get their own type, and you'll see + do something completely different with them.

summaryBullets: You can now explain what a data type is: a category of data that decides what you're allowed to do with it.
You can now create string variables using single or double quotes and print what's stored inside them.
You can now combine strings with + and control exactly where the spaces land.
You can now predict a program's output before pressing Run — and catch mismatches when they happen.
openingLine: Hey {name} — everything you'll ever build toward {goal} runs on one quiet idea: computers treat text as its own kind of thing, called a string. Today you'll create strings, store them, and glue them together like Lego bricks. Quick warm-up: if Python glues "sun" and "shine" with +, what do you think comes out?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you made print() juggle multiple values with commas, + and * — now you'll learn what those text values actually are and how to store and combine them yourself.

dialogueExemplars.title: Confident but wrong about the space in concatenation

turns.text: Easy — print("Hello," + "Ada") prints Hello, Ada. The + adds the space, obviously.
I love the confidence — and you're one character away from right. Here's the catch: + glues strings with absolutely nothing in between. So where would a space have to live for it to show up?
Oh... inside the quotes? Like "Hello, " with a space at the end?
Exactly — the space is part of the string, not a gift from +. Type both versions in the editor and press Run: can you spot the difference in the output?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need strings?

turns.text: Honestly, why do I need this? It's just text.
Fair question! Every time an app says "Hi, Sam!" or "Order #382 shipped", that's string concatenation — a fixed piece glued to a piece that changes. It's everywhere. Want to build one right now?
Tiny task: make a variable name with your own name in quotes, then print("Hi, " + name + "!"). Type it in the editor and press Run — what do you predict it'll say?

dialogueExemplars.title: Absolute beginner: I don't know anything about coding

turns.text: I have to be honest, I don't really know anything about coding. What even is a string?
That's the perfect place to start, truly. A string is just text wrapped in quotes so the computer knows it's text — like "hello". Ready for the tiniest possible first step?
Type print("hello") in the editor and press Run. That one line makes you someone who works with strings — what appeared in the output?
